discoverer: Ida Noddack, Walter Noddack & Otto Berg
named_by: Ida Noddack, Walter Noddack & Otto Berg
etymology: From Rhenus — the Latin name for the Rhine River, which flows through Germany where the discovery was made. The Noddacks chose a geographical name honoring their homeland, following the well-established tradition in element naming. The Rhine is one of Europe's great rivers and a symbol of German culture and history.

timeline.event: Mendeleev predicts two missing elements — 'eka-manganese' (element 75) and 'dvi-manganese' (element 43, technetium). Both lie in the same period as manganese but heavier. The search for these two holes in the periodic table occupies chemists for decades.

Masataka Ogawa claims to discover element 75 in thorianite, naming it 'nipponium' — the claim is not confirmed. The true element 75 proves extraordinarily difficult to find because it occurs in concentrations of parts per billion in molybdenite ore.

Ida Noddack, Walter Noddack, and Otto Berg, using X-ray spectroscopy at the Physikalisch-Technische Reichsanstalt in Berlin, detect both element 75 and what they claim is element 43 in columbite, gadolinite, and molybdenite ores. Their element 75 claim proves correct; their element 43 claim is not reproducible.

The team names element 75 rhenium after the Rhine River and processes 660 kg of molybdenite to extract 1 gram of rhenium — demonstrating both the element's genuine existence and its extraordinary scarcity in nature.

Ida and Walter Noddack produce 1 gram of rhenium metal — at the time the world's entire purified supply — and characterize it as a dense, silvery metal with a very high melting point (3186°C). Rhenium becomes the last naturally occurring non-radioactive element to be discovered.

Ida Noddack, in a remarkable scientific paper often overlooked, suggests that when uranium is bombarded with neutrons it might split into large fragments rather than simply emitting alpha particles — anticipating nuclear fission nine years before Hahn, Strassmann, and Meitner's discovery. Her proposal is dismissed at the time.

Rhenium-platinum bimetallic catalysts are developed for catalytic reforming of naphtha — the process that converts crude naphtha to high-octane gasoline. The Pt-Re catalyst extends catalyst life dramatically and allows lower operating pressures, and it remains the dominant technology for gasoline production refineries to this day.

story: Rhenium was the last naturally occurring non-radioactive element to be discovered — found in 1925 only because X-ray spectroscopy had finally given chemists a tool sensitive enough to detect it in vanishingly small concentrations. Ida Noddack, who was central to the discovery, also made a prescient suggestion about nuclear fission that the scientific community dismissed because it came from a woman without the institutional backing to press her case. Her prediction was essentially correct — she just lacked the experimental framework to prove it. Rhenium's modern importance — as the element that makes jet engine turbine blades withstand temperatures approaching their melting point — is invisible to the public but fundamental to modern aviation.
source_note: Discovered by Ida Noddack, Walter Noddack, and Otto Berg (1925) using X-ray spectroscopy. Named after Rhine River (Rhenus). Published in Naturwissenschaften, 1925.
